title: Enable Dracut FIPS Module
triage_reason: Enabling the dracut FIPS module changes the kernel boot configuration to enforce FIPS mode, which is not supported on an already installed system and can render the system unbootable or lock out remote access. The policy explicitly classifies FIPS changes as too dangerous to remediate automatically.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; Potential boot failure; Loss of SSH/console access; System instability due to unsupported FIPS enablement on existing installation

title: Enable FIPS Mode
triage_reason: Enabling FIPS mode requires kernel boot parameter changes and cryptographic policy updates that are not supported on an already installed system. Applying this change can cause boot failures, loss of SSH/console access, and incompatibility with existing services, making remediation high risk.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; authentication/authorization affected; potential boot failure; loss of SSH/console access; cryptographic incompatibility with existing applications

title: Set kernel parameter 'crypto.fips_enabled' to 1
triage_reason: Enabling FIPS mode requires adding the fips=1 kernel boot parameter, which is only supported at installation time. Changing this on an existing system is not supported and can prevent the system from booting or cause compliance issues. According to policy, FIPS and bootloader/kernel parameter changes are classified as too dangerous to remediate automatically.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; FIPS mode activation; kernel boot parameter modification; potential system boot failure; non‑compliance with FIPS requirements

title: The Installed Operating System Is Vendor Supported
triage_reason: Remediation requires verifying the OS version and potentially performing an OS upgrade to a supported release. This can introduce service disruptions, compatibility issues, and may require a reboot, thus a human decision and planning are needed.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; Potential service downtime during upgrade; Compatibility issues with existing applications; Loss of custom configuration if not backed up; Reboot may interrupt remote access

title: Ensure /var/tmp Located On Separate Partition
triage_reason: Filesystem/partition/mount-option changes can break boot or services and should not be auto-remediated.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; filesystems affected; filesystems/partitioning; service disruption risk

title: Uninstall gssproxy Package
triage_reason: Removing gssproxy may break services that rely on GSSAPI authentication (e.g., SSH, Kerberos) and can cause dependency failures. The remediation script warns about dependent packages, indicating a non‑trivial risk of service disruption or loss of authentication capability, which warrants human validation before proceeding.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; dependency breakage; potential authentication failure; service disruption

title: Ensure Red Hat GPG Key Installed
triage_reason: The remediation only imports a trusted GPG key and optionally verifies directory permissions. This operation does not affect authentication, networking, or critical system services and is a standard STIG step for package verification.
review_feedback: The plan now addresses the earlier concerns: it checks directory permissions, validates the key file's existence, verifies the fingerprint against known values, avoids duplicate imports, and only imports when needed. This should remediate the OpenSCAP finding on a correctly configured system.
review_concerns: The command `dnf reinstall -y redhat-release` may not exist or be appropriate on Rocky Linux, potentially causing the script to fail if the package is unavailable.; The plan does not explicitly handle the case where the fingerprint verification fails (e.g., aborting with an error).; It assumes the `gpg` tool is installed; if not, the fingerprint check would error out.

title: Modify the System Login Banner
triage_reason: The remediation only requires editing a plain text file (/etc/issue) to display a legal disclaimer. This change does not affect authentication mechanisms, networking, or boot processes, and poses no risk of locking out SSH or console access.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; compliance; audit
review_feedback: The plan safely backs up and updates the banner files, but the disclaimer text is truncated and does not match the required DoD banner, so the OpenSCAP rule will likely still fail.
review_concerns: Disclaimer text is incomplete and does not include the full DoD required message.; Using a partial snippet may not satisfy the compliance check.; No verification step to ensure the banner meets the exact required pattern.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Digit Characters
triage_reason: The remediation only modifies the PAM pwquality configuration to enforce a digit requirement. This is a standard STIG hardening step, does not affect system boot or critical services, and is unlikely to cause lockout if tested before deployment.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential for users to be unable to set passwords that meet the new digit requirement; Misconfiguration could weaken password policy if set incorrectly
review_feedback: The plan correctly enforces a minimum of one digit by setting dcredit = -1, which resolves the vulnerability in the typical case. However, it does not address possible existing dcredit settings in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf files that could override the main configuration, nor does it back up files before editing. Adding those steps would make the remediation more robust.
review_concerns: Existing dcredit entries in pwquality.conf.d may override the setting applied to pwquality.conf.; No backup of configuration files before in‑place editing.; The grep check does not differentiate between commented and active lines when deciding whether to append.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly sets dcredit = -1 to require at least one digit, which is the intended remediation. Editing pwquality.conf is low‑risk and does not affect PAM stack logic beyond password complexity, so it will not lock out existing users or break critical services.
qa_side_effects: If existing dcredit settings exist in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/, they may override the change; No backup of the original configuration file is made before in‑place editing

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Enforce for root User
triage_reason: The remediation only adds a single PAM configuration directive (enforce_for_root) to /etc/security/pwquality.conf, a standard STIG hardening step. It does not modify kernel, bootloader, or filesystem layout, and the change is low‑complexity. While there is a minor risk that the existing root password may not meet the new complexity rules, this can be mitigated by ensuring the password complies before applying the change.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Root password may not meet new complexity requirements, potentially causing authentication failure for root
remedy_commands_executed: cat /etc/security/pwquality.conf; rpm -q libpwquality; sed -i -e '$a\' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; cp /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak; sed -i '/^[ \t]*#\?\s*enforce_for_root/d' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; printf '%s\n' 'enforce_for_root' >> /etc/security/pwquality.conf; cat /etc/security/pwquality.conf
review_feedback: The plan correctly adds the enforce_for_root directive and removes any existing entries, which will enable password complexity enforcement for root. It creates a backup and ensures the file ends with a newline, which are good safety measures. Minor improvements could be made for robustness.
review_concerns: The sed delete pattern is case‑sensitive; if the line uses different case it may not be removed.; The plan does not verify the presence of libpwquality (the pwquality PAM module) before editing, which could lead to unnecessary changes on systems without the module.; Appending the directive without an explicit value relies on the default behavior; using "enforce_for_root = 1" would be clearer.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only modifies /etc/security/pwquality.conf to add the enforce_for_root directive, which is the intended fix for the vulnerability. The changes are limited to a configuration file used by the pwquality PAM module and do not affect PAM stack ordering, authentication mechanisms, or active services. Creating a backup and ensuring a newline before appending are safe practices. The only impact is that future root password changes will be subject to the module's complexity rules, which is expected behavior.
qa_side_effects: Root password changes will now be required to meet pwquality complexity rules, which could cause a password change to fail if the new password does not comply.; If libpwquality is not installed, the added directive will have no effect but will not cause harm.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Lowercase Characters
triage_reason: The remediation only edits a PAM password quality configuration file to enforce a lowercase character requirement. This is a standard STIG hardening step, does not affect critical services, and is unlikely to cause lockout if the change is applied correctly.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential for user password lockout if the policy becomes too restrictive; Misconfiguration could allow weak passwords if the setting is omitted
remedy_commands_executed: cp /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak; ls /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d 2>/dev/null || echo 'no dir'; sed -i 's/^[[:space:]]*lcredit[[:space:]]*=.*/lcredit = -1/' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; grep -R "lcredit" -n /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d 2>/dev/null || echo 'none'
review_feedback: The plan correctly sets lcredit = -1 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf, which satisfies the requirement for at least one lowercase character. However, it does not consider possible overrides in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf and does not create a backup before editing, which are minor gaps in robustness.
review_concerns: Only modifies /etc/security/pwquality.conf; existing lcredit settings in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf could override the change.; No backup of the original configuration file is made before the in‑place edit.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only changes the lcredit parameter to -1, which is the correct way to require at least one lowercase character. The edit targets a single configuration file with a simple sed replace or append, and will not affect existing passwords. While it does not handle possible overrides in pwquality.conf.d or create a backup, the change is unlikely to break critical services and should not lock out SSH access.
qa_side_effects: Existing passwords that lack lowercase characters will remain valid until changed, but users may be forced to choose a new password with a lowercase letter on next password change.; If the sed command corrupts the file syntax, pam_pwquality could fail, potentially preventing all PAM password authentication (SSH, sudo, su).

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Length
triage_reason: The remediation only edits PAM password quality configuration files to set the minlen parameter. This is a standard STIG hardening step, does not affect boot or critical services, and carries minimal risk of lockout if the value is reasonable. Therefore it is safe to apply automatically.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential user password lockout if minlen is set excessively high; Incorrect syntax could prevent PAM from loading the module

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Special Characters
triage_reason: The remediation only edits pam_pwquality configuration to set ocredit, a standard password policy change. It does not affect authentication mechanisms, bootloader, or filesystems, and follows typical STIG hardening. Risk of lockout is minimal.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; potential for users to need to update passwords to meet new special character requirement
review_feedback: The plan will set ocredit = -1, which enforces at least one special character and removes conflicting settings in .conf.d files, thus addressing the vulnerability.
review_concerns: The sed replacement may unintentionally modify commented lines, leaving a stray comment character that could disable the setting.; No backup of the original configuration files is performed before editing.; The plan does not verify that pam_pwquality is the active module on the system; on some distributions pam_cracklib may be used instead.; No validation step after edit to ensure the syntax is correct and the setting is effective.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly updates pwquality.conf to enforce at least one special character (ocredit=-1) and removes any conflicting settings in pwquality.conf.d, which aligns with the vulnerability remediation. This change only affects password quality checks for future password changes and does not modify PAM module ordering, so it is unlikely to break sshd, login, or other PAM‑based services or cause immediate lock‑outs.
qa_side_effects: Password changes that lack a special character will be rejected; Users will need to create new passwords meeting the special‑character requirement

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Uppercase Characters
triage_reason: Adjusting the ucredit parameter in pwquality.conf is a standard PAM password policy change that does not affect system boot, networking, or filesystem layout. It is low‑risk and aligns with typical STIG hardening practices.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential user inconvenience if passwords do not meet new uppercase requirement; Possible password reset requests
remedy_commands_executed: test -d /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d && echo exists || echo not_exists; sed -i 's/^[[:space:]]*ucredit[[:space:]]*=.*/ucredit = -1/' /etc/security/pwquality.conf
review_feedback: The plan correctly removes conflicting drop‑in settings and enforces the required ucredit=-1 value in the main pwquality.conf, which will satisfy the vulnerability requirement. Minor wording and regex issues are noted, but they do not prevent the fix.
review_concerns: The sed regex used to delete lines in the drop‑in files may not match lines that are commented without a space after the # character.; The plan assumes the drop‑in directory exists and may fail silently if no *.conf files are present.; The approach does """""""""""""""""""""""""""""""""""""""""""""""""""""""""""""""
qa_verdict_reason: The remediation only edits pwquality configuration to require at least one uppercase character. This does not affect existing password verification, only future password changes, and does not modify PAM stack ordering or critical services. Deleting stray ucredit lines from drop‑in files and enforcing the correct setting in the main file is a standard, low‑risk hardening step.
qa_side_effects: Future password changes will require an uppercase character, which may cause password‑change scripts that do not meet this rule to fail.; If the sed regex does not match a commented line, a duplicate ucredit setting could remain in a drop‑in file, but this is harmless.

title: Configure Logind to terminate idle sessions after certain time of inactivity
triage_reason: The remediation only edits /etc/systemd/logind.conf to set StopIdleSessionSec=, which is a standard, low‑impact configuration change that does not affect authentication, bootloader, or filesystem layout. It does not risk locking out SSH or console access.
triage_estimated_impact: Potential need to restart systemd-logind service; Misconfiguration could leave sessions running longer than intended
review_feedback: The proposed sed command only operates on the line that contains the [Login] header, so it will not replace an existing StopIdleSessionSec line elsewhere in the section and will always append a new line after the header. If the [Login] section is absent, nothing will be added, leaving the vulnerability unaddressed. Additionally, the script can create duplicate entries and does not verify whether the setting already exists. These shortcomings mean the plan may not reliably remediate the issue.
review_concerns: Sed address '/^\[Login\]/' limits the block to the header line, so existing StopIdleSessionSec entries are not replaced.; If the [Login] section is missing, the command makes no changes, leaving the configuration unchanged.; The command will always append a new StopIdleSessionSec line, potentially creating duplicate entries.; No check is performed to ensure only one StopIdleSessionSec line exists in the file.; Reloading systemd-logind may not apply the change if the service is not reloaded correctly.

title: Only Authorized Local User Accounts Exist on Operating System
triage_reason: Remediation involves deleting user accounts. If an unauthorized account is actually required for system or application functionality, removal could cause service disruption or loss of access, especially if the account is used for remote login (SSH) or console access. Human verification is needed to ensure only truly unnecessary accounts are removed.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential removal of needed service accounts; Possible loss of SSH or console access; Impact on applications relying on specific local users

title: Ensure All User Initialization Files Have Mode 0740 Or Less Permissive
triage_reason: The remediation only adjusts file permissions on user initialization files, which is a standard STIG hardening step and does not affect authentication mechanisms, networking, or critical services. The risk of lockout or system breakage is minimal.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; file permission change; potential impact on user-specific startup scripts

title: Ensure the Default Umask is Set Correctly in /etc/profile
triage_reason: The remediation only edits or adds a simple umask line in /etc/profile and scripts under /etc/profile.d. This change does not affect authentication, network services, or boot configuration and is unlikely to cause lockout or service disruption.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected

title: Set the Boot Loader Admin Username to a Non-Default Value
triage_reason: The remediation requires editing the GRUB2 boot loader configuration and regenerating the boot menu. Misconfiguration can render the system unbootable or lock out console access, which aligns with the policy that bootloader changes are considered too dangerous to remediate automatically.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; authentication/authorization affected; Potential boot failure; Loss of console access; Inability to modify boot parameters without rescue media

title: Set Boot Loader Password in grub2
triage_reason: The remediation modifies the GRUB2 bootloader configuration to add a superuser password. Incorrect configuration can render the system unbootable or lock out administrators, which aligns with the policy that bootloader changes are considered too risky for automated remediation.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; authentication/authorization affected; bootloader misconfiguration; potential system unbootable; password lockout; need for physical console access for recovery

title: Disable Kernel Parameter for IPv4 Forwarding on all IPv4 Interfaces
triage_reason: The remediation only edits sysctl configuration files to disable IPv4 forwarding and applies the change at runtime. This is a standard, low‑impact configuration change that does not affect authentication, bootloader, or filesystem layout, and it follows the policy's safe_to_remediate criteria for sysctl persistence.
triage_estimated_impact: networking affected; Disabling IPv4 forwarding may disrupt services that rely on routing or NAT; Potential temporary loss of network connectivity if the system is currently acting as a router
review_feedback: Review output was not valid JSON. Raw: ...
review_concerns: Could not parse LLM response

title: Ensure All World-Writable Directories Are Owned by root User
triage_reason: Changing ownership of all world-writable directories to root can impact services that rely on specific directory ownership (e.g., web servers, application runtimes). A blind bulk change may cause service failures, so a manual review of each directory is advisable before remediation.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Potential service disruption; Incorrect ownership for application-specific directories; Unintended impact on containerized or network-mounted filesystems

title: Verify that All World-Writable Directories Have Sticky Bits Set
triage_reason: Applying the sticky bit to world-writable directories is a standard permission hardening measure that does not affect authentication, boot process, or critical services. The remediation uses a find/chmod command, which is low risk for lockout. While some applications may have specific directory requirements, the change is reversible and can be scoped.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Application-specific directories may rely on non-sticky behavior; Potential for unintended permission changes if the find command is mis-scoped

title: Verify Permissions on Backup group File
triage_reason: The remediation only changes file permissions on a backup copy of /etc/group, a simple chmod operation that does not affect active authentication mechanisms, networking, or boot configuration. Such changes are standard STIG hardening steps and are unlikely to cause lockout or service disruption.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Incorrect permissions could expose group information or allow unintended modifications
review_feedback: The plan correctly sets the backup /etc/group- permissions to 0644, which addresses the OpenSCAP finding. The steps are safe and straightforward, though they could be streamlined.
qa_verdict_reason: Changing the backup file /etc/group- to mode 0644 matches the security recommendation and does not impact any running services or authentication mechanisms. The backup file is not used by the system during normal operation, so the change is safe.

title: Verify Permissions on Backup gshadow File
triage_reason: The remediation only changes file permissions on a backup gshadow file, a standard STIG hardening step that does not affect system boot or critical services. The change is low risk and aligns with policy for safe file permission fixes.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; filesystems affected; potential impact on group authentication data if permissions are set incorrectly
review_feedback: The plan will correctly set /etc/gshadow- permissions to 0000, addressing the vulnerability. It includes a preliminary read to verify the file exists, which is safe but could be simplified. Ensure the command runs with root privileges and verify the file is a regular file before changing permissions.
review_concerns: Reading the file unnecessarily exposes its contents to the remediation script logs.; No explicit check that the file is a regular file; if a symlink exists it could affect another path.
qa_verdict_reason: Setting /etc/gshadow- permissions to 0000 matches the recommendation and does not affect the active /etc/gshadow file used for authentication. The backup file is not consulted by login or PAM, so services such as sshd, auditd, firewalld, and networking will continue to operate normally. The only minor risk is reading the file could log its contents, but this does not impact system functionality.
qa_side_effects: Reading /etc/gshadow- may expose its contents in remediation logs; If /etc/gshadow- were a symlink, chmod could affect the linked target

title: Verify Permissions on Backup passwd File
triage_reason: Changing the permissions of the backup passwd file is a simple file permission adjustment that does not affect active authentication mechanisms or critical services, making it low risk and straightforward to remediate.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected
review_feedback: The plan correctly sets the owner to root:root and permissions to 0644, which satisfies the vulnerability requirement. Execution order and separate commands follow policy, and no harmful actions are introduced.
review_concerns: Reading the file may unnecessarily expose its contents in logs or console output.; The plan does not verify the current state before making changes, which could cause unnecessary writes.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan sets the backup passwd file to root:root ownership and 0644 permissions, which matches the security requirement and does not affect the active /etc/passwd used for authentication, so it is unlikely to disrupt services or lock out access.
qa_side_effects: Reading the file may log its contents in command history or audit logs; Unnecessary chown/chmod if the file already has the correct attributes

title: Verify Permissions on Backup shadow File
triage_reason: Changing permissions on a backup shadow file to remove all access is a straightforward file permission change that does not affect active authentication files or services. The fix follows standard STIG guidance and poses no risk of lockout or service disruption.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; filesystems affected
review_feedback: The plan will correctly set the backup shadow file permissions to 0000, fixing the finding. Using the symbolic mode recommended by the STIG would be clearer, and verifying the file's existence with a read operation could expose its contents. A safer check would use a test for existence rather than reading the step.
review_concerns: Reading the file contents may expose sensitive data in logs or audit trails.; The plan does not verify the current permissions before changing them.; Using 0000 octal mode is functionally equivalent but deviates from the STIG‑like symbolic notation.; No ​   ​ ​     ​    ...  ... … … … … …
qa_verdict_reason: Setting /etc/shadow- to mode 0000 removes all non‑root access as required by the STIG and does not affect system authentication or any critical services; root can still read the file. The only minor concern is that reading the file may log sensitive data, but this does not cause service disruption.
qa_side_effects: Potential exposure of backup shadow contents in audit or logging output when the file is read; No change to existing permissions before modification, so no confirmation step

title: Verify Permissions on group File
triage_reason: Changing /etc/group permissions to 0644 is a standard STIG file permission fix that does not affect authentication, authorization, networking, or critical services, and poses no risk of lockout.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected
review_feedback: The plan correctly sets /etc/group to 0644, which satisfies the STIG requirement and does not impact system functionality.
qa_verdict_reason: Setting /etc/group to 0644 (rw-r--r--) matches default system permissions and does not interfere with authentication, ssh, or any core services. The plan only reads the file and applies a standard chmod, which is safe and reversible.

title: Verify Permissions on gshadow File
triage_reason: Changing /etc/gshadow permissions to 0000 or the recommended restrictive mode is a standard STIG hardening step that does not affect system boot, networking, or authentication mechanisms directly. It reduces exposure of shadow password data without risking SSH or console lockout.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential misconfiguration if incorrect chmod syntax is used; If permissions are set too restrictively, some tools that read gshadow may fail
remedy_commands_executed: chown root:shadow /etc/gshadow; getent group shadow; ls -l /etc/gshadow; groupadd shadow; chown root:shadow /etc/gshadow; chmod 0000 /etc/gshadow
review_feedback: The plan correctly ensures the shadow group exists, sets ownership to root:shadow, and restricts access to /etc/gshadow. However, the chmod symbolic mode used (u-xwrs,g-xwrs,o-xwrt) unintentionally sets the setuid, setgid, and sticky bits, resulting in mode 7000 instead of the intended 0000. This does not match the STIG requirement and could be considered non‑standard.
review_concerns: Using symbolic mode with 's' and 't' adds setuid, setgid, and sticky bits, which are unnecessary and deviate from the required 0000 permission
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly ensures the shadow group exists, sets ownership to root:shadow, and removes all permissions from /etc/gshadow (resulting in mode 0000). This matches standard STIG requirements and does not interfere with system services or authentication mechanisms.

title: Verify Permissions on passwd File
triage_reason: Adjusting /etc/passwd to mode 0644 is a standard STIG remediation with no risk of locking out users or breaking critical services.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; filesystems affected
review_feedback: The plan correctly sets /etc/passwd to mode 0644, which resolves the vulnerability. Adding sudo, verifying ownership, and confirming the change would make the remediation more robust.
review_concerns: The command does not explicitly include sudo; if not run as root it will fail.; The plan does not verify that the file owner and group remain root:root after the change.; No post‑remediation verification step is included to confirm the permission change.
qa_verdict_reason: Changing /etc/passwd permissions to 0644 is the standard and required setting. It does not interfere with any running services and will not lock out SSH or break authentication.

title: Verify Permissions on shadow File
triage_reason: Adjusting /etc/shadow permissions follows a standard STIG hardening pattern. Root retains access, and services that require shadow read run as root, so the change is unlikely to cause lockout or system breakage.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; filesystems affected; Incorrect permission bits could prevent non‑root services from reading shadow; Potential temporary authentication issues if applied without verification
review_feedback: Setting /etc/shadow to mode 0000 will prevent all non-root processes (including PAM and other authentication services that may drop privileges) from reading the file, leading to login failures and system lockout. The recommended secure permissions are typically 0640 owned by root:shadow, not 0000. The plan therefore introduces a serious operational risk and does not implement the appropriate remediation.
review_concerns: Mode 0000 removes group read access required by authentication mechanisms that run under non-root accounts.; Root can bypass permissions, but many services access /etc/shadow after dropping root privileges, causing authentication failures.; The plan does not verify file ownership, which should be root:shadow.

title: Verify Group Who Owns /var/log Directory
triage_reason: Changing the group owner of /var/log is a straightforward file permission adjustment that does not affect authentication, networking, or critical services, and follows standard STIG remediation patterns.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; potential misconfiguration if group 0 does not exist; minor impact on log access permissions

title: Verify User Who Owns /var/log Directory
triage_reason: Changing the owner of /var/log to root is a straightforward file permission adjustment that does not affect authentication mechanisms, networking, or critical boot components. The remediation uses a simple chown command, which is low risk and aligns with standard STIG hardening practices.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Improper ownership could allow non‑privileged users to tamper with log files; Incorrect ownership may cause audit or logging failures
review_feedback: The plan correctly changes the ownership of /var/log to root, which resolves the finding. It could be refined to explicitly use sudo, verify the root user exists, and protect against symbolic links, but it will function as intended.
review_concerns: The plan does not verify that the root user (UID 0) exists before attempting chown.; It does not explicitly prevent following symlinks; using --no-dereference would be safer.
qa_verdict_reason: Changing the ownership of /var/log to root is the standard configuration and does not interfere with system services. The command targets only the top‑level directory, avoids recursive changes, and the directory is normally owned by root anyway, so the fix resolves the finding without causing outages or lockouts.
qa_side_effects: Change of /var/log directory owner to root

title: Verify Permissions on /var/log Directory
triage_reason: The remediation only changes directory permissions on /var/log, which is a standard STIG hardening step. It does not affect authentication, networking, or boot processes, and the change is reversible. The risk of lockout or service disruption is minimal.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Incorrect permissions could expose log data; Overly restrictive permissions might prevent services from writing logs
review_feedback: The plan correctly sets /var/log to 0755, which removes unwanted setuid/setgid/sticky bits and satisfies the STIG requirement. Using a direct chmod is simpler than the more complex find command in the recommendation.
review_concerns: The plan does not explicitly verify removal of setuid, setgid, and sticky bits beyond the numeric mode check.; Potential for transient permission issues if services are writing to /var/log at the exact moment of change.
qa_verdict_reason: Setting /var/log to 0755 removes unexpected setuid/setgid/sticky bits and matches the default permission model for log directories on RHEL/Rocky. This change does not affect authentication mechanisms, networking, or firewall services and is unlikely to cause service outages. At worst, a brief race condition could occur if a process is writing a log at the exact moment the mode is altered, but this does not constitute a critical failure.
qa_side_effects: Transient permission mismatch if a process writes to /var/log during the chmod operation; Potential need to restart logging services (e.g., rsyslog, journald) to recognize the new permissions, though not required

title: Verify Permissions on /var/log/messages File
triage_reason: Changing /var/log/messages permissions to 0600 is a standard file permission hardening step that does not affect authentication, networking, or critical system services, and poses minimal risk of lockout or service disruption.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Potential loss of log readability for non‑root users
review_feedback: The plan correctly sets /var/log/messages to mode 0600, matching the OpenSCAP rule. It includes verification steps before and after the change.
qa_verdict_reason: Setting /var/log/messages to mode 0600 restricts access to root only, which is the default and required by the OpenSCAP rule. The rsyslog daemon and logrotate run as root, so they will continue to write to the file without issue. No critical services are dependent on non‑root read access, so the change will not cause outages or lockouts.
qa_side_effects: Non‑root users will no longer be able to read the messages log; Any custom scripts that relied on group/world read permissions will lose access

title: Add nosuid Option to /boot/efi
triage_reason: The remediation modifies the mount options of the /boot/efi partition, which is part of the boot filesystem. According to policy, any changes to partitioning, filesystem, or bootloader settings are classified as too dangerous to remediate automatically because they can affect system bootability.

title: Disable the use of user namespaces
triage_reason: The remediation consists of setting a kernel sysctl parameter and persisting it in /etc/sysctl.d, which is a standard, low‑complexity STIG action. It does not affect authentication, networking, or filesystem layout and does not risk loss of SSH or console access. The only impact is on container workloads that rely on user namespaces, which can be evaluated separately.
triage_estimated_impact: Potential disruption of container workloads that require user namespaces
review_feedback: The plan correctly sets and persists user.max_user_namespaces=0, backs up any existing config, and reloads sysctl. It addresses the prior review concerns. Minor improvements: ensure the backup is timestamped and verify the sysctl reload succeeded.
review_concerns: None
qa_verdict_reason: The remediation correctly sets and persists user.max_user_namespaces=0 with proper backup and sysctl reload. Core system services (sshd, auditd, firewalld, networking) do not depend on user namespaces, so they will continue to function. The change only affects workloads that rely on unprivileged user namespaces.
qa_side_effects: Unprivileged container runtimes (e.g., podman, Docker) will be unable to create containers; Sandboxed applications that use user namespaces (e.g., bubblewrap, Flatpak, Snap) may fail to start

title: Restrict Exposed Kernel Pointer Addresses Access
triage_reason: The remediation only edits or adds a sysctl setting and comments out existing entries. This is a standard, low‑impact configuration change that does not affect authentication, networking, or boot processes.
triage_estimated_impact: Potential for mis‑typed sysctl value; Existing custom sysctl configurations may be overwritten
remedy_commands_executed: ls -1 /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf 2>/dev/null || true; ls -1 /run/sysctl.d/*.conf 2>/dev/null || true; sed -i '/^[[:space:]]*kernel\.kptr_restrict[[:space:]]*=.*$/ s/^/# /' /etc/sysctl.conf; sed -i '/^[[:space:]]*kernel\.kptr_restrict[[:space:]]*=.*$/ s/^/# /' /etc/sysctl.d/99-sysctl.conf; sysctl --system
review_feedback: The plan correctly comments out any existing kernel.kptr_restrict entries, adds a persistent configuration file that sets the value to 2, and reloads sysctl settings, satisfying the vulnerability requirement and addressing prior review feedback.
review_concerns: If another sysctl file with a higher lexical order sets kernel.kptr_restrict, it could override the 99-zz file.; The plan assumes the directory processing order; verify that no later file overrides the setting.
qa_verdict_reason: Setting kernel.kptr_restrict to 2 is a standard hardening measure that does not affect core services. Commenting existing entries and adding a new high‑order sysctl file is safe; the reload via sysctl --system applies the change without disrupting running daemons.
qa_side_effects: If another sysctl file with a later lexical order sets kernel.kptr_restrict to a different value, it could override the intended setting.; Debugging or profiling tools that rely on kernel pointer visibility may be limited.

title: Enable Randomized Layout of Virtual Address Space
triage_reason: The remediation only edits or adds a sysctl configuration file to set kernel.randomize_va_space=2, which is a standard STIG hardening step. It does not affect authentication, networking, or filesystem layout, and the change takes effect at runtime without requiring a reboot. The risk of lockout or service disruption is negligible.
review_feedback: The plan will set the kernel.randomize_va_space value to 2 at runtime and make it persistent via a dedicated sysctl file. It addresses the vulnerability, but it could be improved by reloading sysctl settings with sysctl --system and ensuring proper permissions.
review_concerns: The plan does not reload the new sysctl configuration after creating the file, so the persistent setting won't take effect until a reboot.; Using sed without anchoring the exact parameter may comment out lines that contain the string in comments or other contexts.; The echo command writes directly to /proc/sys without checking for errors or required privileges.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only modifies sysctl configuration for kernel.randomize_va_space, a non‑critical kernel parameter, and sets the desired value both at runtime and persistently. The sed command safely comments out only active settings, and the echo to /proc/sys updates the current kernel state without affecting services. No services such as sshd, firewalld, or auditd rely on this setting, so the change will not cause outages or lockouts.
qa_side_effects: Modifies /etc/sysctl.conf and any /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf files to comment out existing kernel.randomize_va_space entries; Creates a new /etc/sysctl.d/99-kernel_randomize_va_space.conf file; Persistent change will only take effect after a reboot or a manual sysctl reload

title: Elevate The SELinux Context When An Administrator Calls The Sudo Command
triage_reason: The remediation consists of adding a single, well‑documented rule to a file in /etc/sudoers.d using visudo. This is a standard STIG hardening step for sudo/SELinux integration, does not require a reboot, and poses minimal risk of system lockout if the syntax is validated with visudo before saving.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Syntax error in sudoers file could prevent sudo usage; Incorrect SELinux type/role could cause denied operations
review_feedback: The plan correctly adds the required SELinux sudo rule, validates syntax with visudo, and sets proper permissions. It will resolve the vulnerability if executed as described.
review_concerns: The plan assumes the admin group is %wheel; verify this matches the environment's designated sudoers admin group.; The SELinux file context for the new sudoers.d file is not explicitly set; the file should have the sudoers_t type to avoid SELinux denials.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan adds the required SELinux sudo rule using a dedicated sudoers.d file, validates syntax with visudo, and sets correct permissions. This is the appropriate remediation and is unlikely to disrupt critical services or lock out access. The only minor omission is an explicit restorecon to ensure the file has the sudoers_t SELinux type, which would not cause a failure, only a possible ignore of the rule.
qa_side_effects: Adds a rule allowing wheel members to run commands with SELinux type sysadm_t and role sysadm_r, which could be leveraged for higher privileges if misused; File may initially lack the sudoers_t SELinux context until a restorecon is performed

title: Ensure SELinux State is Enforcing

title: A remote time server for Chrony is configured

title: Configure Time Service Maxpoll Interval
triage_reason: The remediation only edits NTP/chrony configuration files to set or add a maxpoll value. This is a straightforward text substitution/addition that does not affect authentication, bootloader, or filesystem layout. The change is low risk and can be rolled back by restoring the original config files. Service restart may be required but no reboot.
triage_estimated_impact: networking affected; Syntax error in config could prevent NTP/chrony from starting; Incorrect maxpoll value may affect time synchronization accuracy
remedy_commands_executed: rpm -q chrony; rpm -q ntp; find /etc/chrony.d -type f -name "*.conf"; sed -i -E 's/^(\s*\(server\|pool\|peer\)[^#]*\b)maxpoll[[:space:]]+[0-9]+/\1maxpoll 10/' /etc/chrony.conf; sed -i '/^\s*\(server\|pool\|peer\)\b/ {/^\s*#/! { /maxpoll/! s/$/ maxpoll 10/ } }' /etc/chrony.conf; systemctl restart chronyd
review_feedback: The plan correctly identifies the installed time service, locates the relevant configuration files, and ensures a maxpoll value of 10 is set or added. Restarting the service will apply the changes, so the vulnerability will be resolved.
review_concerns: The sed commands may also modify lines that are commented out, potentially altering documentation or example entries.; No backup of the original configuration files is created before in‑place editing, which could make rollback harder if an error occurs.; The plan assumes only one time service is active; if both chrony and ntpd are installed simultaneously, both will be modified which may not be intended.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly identifies the active time service, modifies only the relevant server/pool/peer entries to set maxpoll 10, and restarts only the time daemon. This does not affect SSH, firewalld, or other core services, and the change is limited to NTP/chrony configuration.
qa_side_effects: Potential modification of commented example lines in config files; No backup of original configuration files before in-place editing; Brief loss of time synchronization during service restart; If both chrony and ntpd are installed, both may be altered and restarted unintentionally

title: Uninstall tftp-server Package
triage_reason: Removing the tftp-server package is a straightforward package removal that does not affect authentication, core system services, or boot configuration. The primary risk is the potential removal of packages that depend on tftp-server, which can be mitigated by checking dependencies before removal.
triage_estimated_impact: possible removal of dependent packages; loss of TFTP service if it is in use
remedy_commands_executed: rpm -q --quiet "tftp-server" && echo installed || echo not_installed; dnf repoquery --whatrequires tftp-server; dnf remove -y --noautoremove "tftp-server"; rpm -q "tftp-server"
review_feedback: The plan correctly removes the tftp-server package and verifies removal. Adding a pre‑removal check for packages that depend on tftp-server would make it safer.

The use of --noautoremove prevents accidental removal of unrelated dependencies, and any reverse‑dependency packages would be removed only if they explicitly require tftp-server, which is rare on production servers. Hence the remediation is unlikely to cause service outages or lockouts.
qa_side_effects: Removal of the tftp-server package; Potential removal of packages that have a hard dependency on tftp-server (e.g., PXE boot or provisioning tools)

title: Enable Certmap in SSSD
triage_reason: Enabling certmap requires adding a specific stanza to /etc/sssd/sssd.conf and restarting the sssd service. This is a straightforward configuration change that does not affect authentication mechanisms or access methods directly, and follows typical STIG hardening patterns.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential misconfiguration could cause SSSD lookup failures; Incorrect matchrule syntax may prevent certificate mapping
review_feedback: The plan correctly adds the required certmap stanza and restarts SSSD, which will address the finding. It could be improved by making the implementation idempotent, handling backup of the original config, and validating the stanza before appending.
review_concerns: The plan overwrites the entire sssd.conf without creating a backup, risking loss of existing configuration if something goes wrong.; It assumes the placeholder domain and rule name match the real environment; using static placeholders may result in an incorrect stanza.; Permissions are forced to 0600 without checking current mode, which is fine but should be verified.; No validation that the added stanza syntax is correct before restarting the service.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan overwrites /etc/sssd/sssd.conf without a backup and blindly appends a certmap stanza using placeholder domain values. If the domain or syntax is incorrect, SSSD may fail to start, causing authentication failures and potential SSH lockout. The lack of validation and idempotency makes the change risky.
qa_side_effects: Potential loss of existing sssd.conf content if write fails; SSSD service could fail to start due to malformed or mismatched stanza; Authentication outage for services that rely on SSSD (e.g., SSH, sudo); Indirect lockout of remote admin access

title: Enable auditd Service
triage_reason: Enabling the auditd service is a standard STIG requirement, involves only starting and enabling a systemd unit, and does not affect authentication, networking, or critical boot components. The remediation script is idempotent and safe to run on a running system.

title: System Audit Logs Must Have Mode 0640 or Less Permissive
triage_reason: Changing the audit log file permission to 0600 is a standard STIG remediation that does not affect system boot, networking, or authentication mechanisms. It only tightens file permissions on a log file, posing minimal risk of lockout or service disruption.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Potential for audit daemon to fail if it cannot write to the log after permission change
remedy_commands_executed: test -f /var/log/audit/audit.log && echo exists || echo missing; chmod 0600 /var/log/audit/audit.log
review_feedback: The plan correctly identifies the audit log location and applies a restrictive 0600 mode, which meets the requirement. Minor gaps such as handling log rotation, verifying file existence, and restarting the audit daemon should be addressed.
review_concerns: Does not ensure permissions are applied to future rotated audit log files; May fail if the audit log file does not exist at the time of chmod; Does not restart or reload auditd to guarantee the daemon uses the new permissions
qa_verdict_reason: Changing the audit log file permissions to 0600 is more restrictive than the required 0640 and does not interfere with auditd's ability to write logs. The operation is limited to a single file, does not modify any running services, and will not lock out SSH or other critical daemons. Minor cosmetic concerns (rotated files, reload) do not constitute a safety risk.
qa_side_effects: Audit log files become unreadable by non‑root users (intended); Future rotated audit logs may inherit default permissions and remain more permissive until rotated again
